quiz_questions.question_text: What is total internal reflection (TIR)?

options.text: Partial reflection only
All light reflected when hitting boundary above the critical angle
Light passing through all materials
Light absorbed at a boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: Total internal reflection occurs when all light is reflected back into the denser medium at angles above the critical angle, with no light passing through the boundary.

quiz_questions.question_text: When does TIR occur?

options.text: At any angle
Only in vacuum
Light goes from less dense to denser medium
Light goes from denser to less dense medium above critical angle

quiz_questions.explanation: TIR occurs when light travels from a denser to a less dense medium at an angle greater than the critical angle. Both conditions must be met.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for the critical angle?

options.text: sin θ_c = n₁/n₂
cos θ_c = n₂/n₁
tan θ_c = n₂/n₁
sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ (where n₁ > n₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: Critical Angle sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ (where n₁ > n₂). At this angle the refracted ray travels along the boundary surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do optical fibers use TIR?

options.text: Light passes straight through
Light is absorbed
Light bounces along the fiber by total internal reflection
Light diffracts inside

quiz_questions.explanation: Optical fibers use TIR to transmit light signals over long distances. Light bounces along the fiber core at angles above the critical angle, keeping it trapped inside.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do diamonds sparkle?

options.text: Very low critical angle causes maximum internal reflection
They are completely transparent to all wavelengths of light
They emit visible light from thermal excitation of atoms
They absorb all colors equally and re-emit white light

quiz_questions.explanation: Diamonds have a very high refractive index (2.42), giving them a very low critical angle (~24°). Most light entering a diamond undergoes total internal reflection multiple times before exiting, creating the sparkle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can TIR occur going from air to glass?

options.text: Yes, but only for red light
Yes, above 42°
Yes, at any angle
No — only from denser to less dense medium

quiz_questions.explanation: TIR can only occur when light travels from a denser medium (higher n) to a less dense medium (lower n). Air to glass is less dense to denser, so TIR cannot happen in that direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Name a medical application of optical fibers.

options.text: Stethoscopes
X-ray machines
Endoscopes
MRI scanners

quiz_questions.explanation: Endoscopes use optical fibers to transmit light and images from inside the body, allowing doctors to examine internal organs without major surgery.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the approximate critical angle for glass (n = 1.5) to air?

options.text: About 24 degrees for standard glass
About 42 degrees for standard glass
About 60 degrees for standard glass
About 90 degrees for standard glass

quiz_questions.explanation: Using sin θ_c = n₂/n₁ = 1/1.5 = 0.667, so θ_c = arcsin(0.667) ≈ 42°. Any light hitting the glass-air boundary at an angle greater than 42° will undergo total internal reflection.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to light at exactly the critical angle?

options.text: The light is completely absorbed by the boundary surface
The light reflects back at a 90-degree angle to the surface
The refracted ray travels along the boundary between the two media
The light passes through without any bending at the boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: At exactly the critical angle, the refracted ray travels along the boundary surface (refracted angle = 90°). Above the critical angle, no refraction occurs and all light is totally internally reflected.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which has a smaller critical angle: diamond (n = 2.42) or glass (n = 1.5)?

options.text: Glass has the smaller critical angle value
Diamond has the smaller critical angle value
Both have exactly the same critical angle value
Neither material can undergo total internal reflection

quiz_questions.explanation: A higher refractive index means a smaller critical angle (sin θ_c = 1/n for a medium-to-air boundary). Diamond (n = 2.42, θ_c ≈ 24°) has a much smaller critical angle than glass (n = 1.5, θ_c ≈ 42°).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'critical angle' in optics?

options.text: The angle of incidence that produces a 90° angle of refraction
The angle at which all light is absorbed by the medium surface
The minimum angle needed for any refraction to take place
The angle at which reflected light has maximum brightness level

quiz_questions.explanation: The critical angle is the specific angle of incidence at which the refracted ray travels exactly along the boundary (90° refraction). Above this angle, total internal reflection occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define 'total internal reflection' in your own terms.

options.text: Some light refracts while the rest is reflected at the boundary
All light is reflected back into the denser medium at the boundary
Light is scattered in random directions at a rough boundary surface
Light is completely absorbed when it strikes a transparent surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Total internal reflection is the complete reflection of light back into the denser medium when it hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle. No light passes through to the other side.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'optical fiber' in telecommunications?

options.text: A copper wire coated in glass that carries electrical current signals
A thin flexible strand of glass that transmits light via TIR
A hollow metal tube that channels radio waves across distances
A plastic rod that converts light energy into electrical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: An optical fiber is a thin, flexible strand of glass or plastic that transmits light signals over long distances using total internal reflection. The core has a higher refractive index than the surrounding cladding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'cladding' mean in an optical fiber?

options.text: The light source attached to the end of the optical fiber
The outer layer with a lower refractive index surrounding the core
The inner core of the fiber where light signals travel forward
The protective plastic jacket on the outside of the fiber cable

quiz_questions.explanation: Cladding is the outer layer of an optical fiber that has a lower refractive index than the core. This difference in refractive indices allows total internal reflection to keep light trapped in the core.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'boundary' mean in the context of TIR?

options.text: The surface where two media with different refractive indices meet
The edge of a mirror where light is no longer reflected properly
The maximum distance that light can travel inside any material
The angle at which the refracted ray disappears from sight

quiz_questions.explanation: In the context of TIR, the boundary is the interface or surface where two different media meet. TIR occurs at this boundary when light in the denser medium hits it above the critical angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by a 'denser medium' in optics?

options.text: A material that weighs more per unit volume than another one
A material that absorbs more light than it allows to pass through
A material with a higher refractive index that slows light more
A material that reflects all light and transmits none at all

quiz_questions.explanation: In optics, a denser medium refers to one with a higher refractive index, where light travels more slowly. This is optical density, not physical density, though they often correlate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'refractive index' tell us about a material?

options.text: How much the material absorbs each color of visible light
How much a material slows light compared to its speed in vacuum
How thick a material is from its front surface to its back
How transparent a material appears to the unaided human eye

quiz_questions.explanation: The refractive index (n = c/v) tells us how much slower light travels in a material compared to a vacuum. A higher refractive index means light is slower and bends more when entering that material.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'endoscope' and how does it work?

options.text: A telescope that uses mirrors to view distant astronomical objects
A medical instrument using optical fibers to see inside the body
A microscope that uses electrons instead of visible light waves
A device that measures the refractive index of different liquids

quiz_questions.explanation: An endoscope is a medical instrument that uses bundles of optical fibers to transmit light into the body and carry images back to the doctor. It relies on total internal reflection within the fibers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'angle of incidence' at a boundary where TIR may occur.

options.text: The angle between the incoming light ray and the boundary surface
The angle between the incoming light ray and the normal to the surface
The angle between the refracted ray and the boundary surface itself
The angle between the reflected ray and the incoming incident ray

quiz_questions.explanation: The angle of incidence is measured between the incoming light ray and the normal (perpendicular line) at the point where the ray meets the boundary. TIR occurs when this angle exceeds the critical angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'total' mean in the term 'total internal reflection'?

options.text: That some light is reflected while the rest passes through the boundary
That the reflection only works for a total of certain wavelengths
That all of the light is reflected with none passing through the boundary
That the reflection happens across the total surface of the material

quiz_questions.explanation: The word 'total' means that 100% of the light is reflected back into the medium — none of it refracts through the boundary. This only happens when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shines a laser from inside a fish tank (glass, n = 1.5) toward the glass-air boundary at 50°. Will TIR occur?

options.text: No, because the angle is less than the critical angle of glass
Yes, because 50° exceeds the critical angle of about 42° for glass
No, because TIR only occurs in optical fibers and not in tanks
Yes, because all light hitting glass at any angle is always reflected

quiz_questions.explanation: The critical angle for glass to air is about 42°. Since the laser hits the boundary at 50° (greater than 42°), and the light is traveling from denser glass to less dense air, TIR will occur.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is designing an optical fiber. They want maximum TIR inside the core. What should they ensure about the core and cladding?

options.text: The core should have a lower refractive index than the cladding
The core should have a higher refractive index than the cladding
The core and cladding should have the same refractive index value
The cladding should be made of metal instead of glass material

quiz_questions.explanation: For TIR to keep light trapped in the fiber core, the core must have a higher refractive index than the cladding. Light traveling in the denser core reflects off the boundary with the less dense cladding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scuba diver looks up at the water surface from below and sees a circular window of light. What causes the dark area around it?

options.text: Algae floating on the water surface blocks the surrounding light
Total internal reflection prevents light from entering at steep angles
The diver's mask creates a shadow that blocks the outer region
Water absorbs all light beyond a certain distance from the diver

quiz_questions.explanation: The diver sees a bright circle (called Snell's window) because light from above can only enter the water within the critical angle cone. Beyond this cone, light undergoes TIR at the surface and the diver sees a mirror-like reflection of the underwater scene.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student gradually increases the angle of a laser beam inside a glass block aimed at the glass-air boundary. What sequence of events occurs?

options.text: The refracted ray bends more, then TIR occurs above the critical angle
The reflected ray disappears and all light passes through the boundary
Nothing changes because the glass always reflects light the same way
The light is absorbed more and more until none exits the glass block

quiz_questions.explanation: As the angle of incidence increases, the refracted ray bends further from the normal. At the critical angle, the refracted ray runs along the boundary. Above the critical angle, no refraction occurs and all light undergoes TIR.

quiz_questions.question_text: A jeweler wants to cut a gemstone to maximize its sparkle. Based on TIR, what should they consider?

options.text: Make the gem as thin as possible so light passes straight through it
Cut angles so light hitting inner surfaces exceeds the critical angle
Polish the gem to be perfectly transparent with no internal reflections
Use a gem with the lowest possible refractive index for best sparkle

quiz_questions.explanation: To maximize sparkle, a jeweler cuts the gem so that light entering from the top strikes internal surfaces at angles greater than the critical angle. This causes multiple total internal reflections before the light exits through the top, creating brilliance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that internet data is transmitted through optical fibers using light. Why is TIR important for this technology?

options.text: TIR keeps light trapped in the fiber so signals travel long distances
TIR converts light signals into electrical signals at each junction
TIR amplifies the light signal so it gets stronger as it travels
TIR changes the color of light to match the data being transmitted

quiz_questions.explanation: TIR is essential for fiber optic communications because it keeps light signals confined within the fiber core. Without TIR, light would leak out at every bounce, and the signal would be lost over short distances.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student shines light from water (n = 1.33) into air. They calculate the critical angle to be about 49°. If they try the same from glass (n = 1.5), will the critical angle be larger or smaller?

options.text: Larger, because glass has a higher refractive index than water
Smaller, because glass has a higher refractive index than water
The same, because the critical angle does not depend on the medium
There is no critical angle for glass because it is a solid material

quiz_questions.explanation: The critical angle decreases as the refractive index of the denser medium increases: sin θ_c = 1/n. Glass (n = 1.5) gives θ_c ≈ 42°, which is smaller than water's θ_c ≈ 49°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a waterproof laser underwater pointing upward at 30° to the normal. Does TIR occur at the water-air surface?

options.text: Yes, because any laser underwater causes total internal reflection
Yes, because 30° is above the critical angle for all materials
No, because 30° is below the critical angle for water (about 49°)
No, because TIR cannot occur at a water-to-air surface boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: The critical angle for water to air is about 49°. Since 30° is less than 49°, the light will refract through the surface into the air rather than undergo total internal reflection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that a prism can redirect light by 90° using TIR instead of a mirror. Why might this be preferred?

options.text: Prisms are always cheaper and smaller than mirrors in all cases
TIR in a prism reflects nearly 100% of light with almost no loss
Prisms can change the color of light while mirrors cannot do so
Mirrors absorb too much light and cannot produce any reflections

quiz_questions.explanation: Total internal reflection in a prism reflects nearly 100% of the light, while a mirror coating typically reflects 90-95% and absorbs or transmits the rest. This makes TIR prisms ideal for high-quality optical instruments like binoculars.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is told that mirages on hot roads are caused by TIR. How does this work?

options.text: Hot air near the road has a lower refractive index, causing light to curve upward via TIR
The road surface acts like a mirror and reflects the sky back to the viewer
Water vapor on the road creates a thin reflective film that bounces light
The heat causes light to travel faster and skip over the road surface area

quiz_questions.explanation: Hot air near the road surface has a lower density and lower refractive index than the cooler air above. Light from the sky traveling downward gradually bends and undergoes TIR near the road, making it appear as if there is water reflecting the sky.
